did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a naive Bayes classifier for distinguishing atypical pigment networks and a two-layer Artificial Neural Network (ANN) for detecting irregular streaks in dermoscopy images of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and testing of image analysis algorithms (histogram-based features with a naive Bayes classifier and shape-based features with a two-layer ANN) for distinguishing atypical pigment networks and detecting irregular streaks in skin lesion images. It includes a Materials and Methods section describing a dataset of 98 images, a Results section reporting specific performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, classification error), and a comparison with competitive approaches, which are characteristic of a research study rather than a review.

title: Nevus atypical pigment network distinction and irregular streaks detection in skin lesions images
journal: SIGNAL PROCESSING ALGORITHMS, ARCHITECTURES, ARRANGEMENTS, AND APPLICATIONS (SPAA) 2015
brief_summary: This study addresses the automated detection of atypical pigment networks and irregular streaks in dermoscopy images to support melanoma diagnosis via the ELM 7-point checklist. The researchers analyzed a dataset of 98 digital skin lesion images, comprising malignant melanomas and non-malignant nevi, acquired using a Nikon D90 camera with a Heine Delta 20 dermatoscope. For atypical pigment network detection, the method utilized histogram-based texture features (specifically the 90th and 99th percentiles) classified by a naive Bayes classifier. Irregular streaks were identified using shape and region-based features processed by a two-layer Artificial Neural Network. The proposed approach achieved a mean sensitivity of 95% and specificity of 100% for atypical networks, and a sensitivity of 91% with a specificity of 97% for irregular streaks. These results indicate that the method offers slightly better performance compared to competitive approaches reported in the literature.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the dataset of 98 digital images of skin nevi; used to support melanoma diagnosis via the ELM 7-point checklist.
Included in the dataset of 98 digital images; specifically, the study distinguishes between atypical and typical pigment networks within nevi, and presence/absence of irregular streaks within nevi.
One of the two specific criteria from the ELM 7-point checklist studied; 19 images with this feature were analyzed.
One of the two specific criteria from the ELM 7-point checklist studied; 25 images with this feature were analyzed.
One of the two specific criteria from the ELM 7-point checklist studied; 22 images with this feature were analyzed.

all_task_types.target: Presence of atypical pigment network (atypical vs. typical)
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task distinguishing between atypical and typical pigment networks using histogram-based texture features (90th and 99th percentiles) and a naive Bayes classifier. Achieved 95% sensitivity and 100% specificity.

all_task_types.target: Presence of irregular streaks (present vs. absent)
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task detecting the presence of irregular streaks using shape and region-based features (solidity, convexity, entropy, skewness) processed by a two-layer Artificial Neural Network (ANN). Achieved 91% sensitivity and 97% specificity.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopy Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of 98 digital dermoscopy images of skin nevi (malignant melanoma and non-malignant) acquired using a Nikon D90 camera equipped with a Heine Delta 20 dermatoscope. Images were recorded in RGB format (16 bits per channel) with a resolution of 2848 x 4288 pixels and a field of view covering 10 by 15 mm. The dataset was curated by a trained dermatologist.

use.training: Used for training the naive Bayes classifier (for atypical network detection) and the two-layer Artificial Neural Network (for irregular streaks detection). The paper notes that results for irregular streaks were obtained on the training data set due to insufficient data for an independent test set.
use.validation: Used for 5-fold cross-validation of the naive Bayes classifier for atypical pigment network detection.
use.testing: Not explicitly used as a separate held-out test set; the authors state that network validation will be performed after acquiring a larger number of images.
use.other: Used to demonstrate the proposed methods and compare results with competitive approaches reported in literature.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To select the best discriminating texture features from a large set of candidates by measuring between-class scatter relative to within-class variance.
To evaluate the performance of the naive Bayes classifier for distinguishing atypical from typical pigment networks.
To classify images as having atypical or typical pigment networks based on histogram-based texture features (90th and 99th percentiles).
To detect the presence of irregular streaks using shape and region-based features (solidity, convexity, entropy, skewness).
To quantify the diagnostic performance of the proposed methods for atypical pigment network detection and irregular streaks detection.
To measure the proportion of misclassified samples for both the naive Bayes classifier (mean classification error) and the ANN (classification error on training data).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper reports performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, classification error) and compares results with literature but does not perform formal statistical hypothesis testing.

Naive Bayes Classifier.details: Averaged result of 5-fold cross-validation. One sample misclassified.

Custom Dermoscopy Dataset (Atypical Pigm.notes: The method used histogram-based texture features (90th and 99th percentiles). The incorrectly classified example showed appearance of both networks in the networks area.

Custom Dermoscopy Dataset (Irregular Str.notes: Results obtained on the training data set due to insufficient number of test images for an independent data set. Features used: solidity, convexity, entropy, and skewness based on distance between contours. Three cases of misclassified nevi were shown; segmentation errors were responsible for misclassification in two cases.

Comparison with Competitive Approaches (.notes: Comparison with results reported in reference [4]. The proposed approach shows slightly better results.

architecture_used.main_model: Two distinct models were used: 1) Naive Bayes classifier for atypical pigment network detection; 2) Two-layer Artificial Neural Network (ANN) for irregular streaks detection.

naive_bayes_classifier.description: Probabilistic classifier used for binary classification of atypical vs. typical pigment networks.
naive_bayes_classifier.input_features: Histogram-based texture features, specifically the 90th and 99th percentiles estimated from the image histogram.

artificial_neural_network.description: Two-layer ANN used for binary classification of irregular streaks vs. no irregular streaks.
artificial_neural_network.architecture: Two layers: Hidden layer with two neurons, and Output layer for classification.
artificial_neural_network.input_features: Four shape and region-based features: solidity, convexity, entropy, and skewness (based on distance between contours).

naive_bayes_classifier.feature_selection: Fisher coefficient used to select the best discriminating parameter from 492 tested textural features.

artificial_neural_network.notes: Results obtained on the training data set due to insufficient number of test images for an independent data set.

naive_bayes_classifier.output: Binary classification (atypical vs. typical pigment network).
naive_bayes_classifier.decision_boundary: Defined by the distribution of the 90th and 99th percentile features.

artificial_neural_network.output: Binary classification (irregular streaks vs. no irregular streaks).

preprocessing_techniques: Correction of image illumination to equalize non-uniform illumination introduced by the dermatoscope
Semiautomatic image segmentation to extract the nevus region
Conversion of original Nikon Electronic Format (NEF) files to 8-bit per channel bitmaps
conclusions: The proposed method for distinguishing atypical pigment networks using histogram-based features (90th and 99th percentiles) and a naive Bayes classifier achieved a mean sensitivity of 95% and specificity of 100% via 5-fold cross-validation.

The proposed method for detecting irregular streaks using shape and region-based features (solidity, convexity, entropy, skewness) and a two-layer Artificial Neural Network achieved a sensitivity of 91% and specificity of 97%.
The results for irregular streaks detection were obtained on the training dataset due to an insufficient number of test images for an independent validation set.

The proposed approach showed slightly better performance compared to competitive approaches reported in the literature (e.g., 95% vs 80% sensitivity for atypical networks; 91% vs 86% sensitivity for irregular streaks).
Misclassifications in irregular streaks detection were attributed to segmentation errors and specific lesion morphologies (e.g., pulled globules) rather than algorithmic failure alone.
The study constitutes a partial implementation of the ELM 7-point checklist, covering only two of the seven criteria (atypical pigment network and irregular streaks).
Future work is required to acquire a larger dataset for independent network validation and to extend the system to cover all seven criteria of the ELM 7-point checklist.
important_remarks: The study is limited to only two of the seven criteria in the ELM 7-point checklist (atypical pigment network and irregular streaks), rather than the full diagnostic protocol.

Results for irregular streaks detection were evaluated on the training dataset (58 examples) due to an insufficient number of test images for an independent validation set, posing a risk of overfitting or optimistic bias.
The dataset is small (98 total images) and curated by a single trained dermatologist, limiting generalizability and introducing potential selection bias.
No external validation was performed; network validation is planned only after acquiring a larger dataset.
Misclassifications in irregular streaks detection were attributed to segmentation errors and specific morphological confounders (e.g., pulled globules) rather than algorithmic failure alone.

The comparison with competitive approaches relies on literature values from reference [4] rather than a direct head-to-head evaluation on the same dataset, limiting the validity of the performance comparison.
